sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE]
Now.
Yeah.
All right.
After when I was liberated, I came to town and the Red Army came in and they knew-- the Soviet government knew-- that the Ukrainian population is-- is against them.
So they decreed that all men up to 55 years old should go to the army, into the Soviet Russian army.
All right?
Since I--
Oh, all-- there were only Ukrainian.
They wanted them in the army, because they were afraid to go into partisan against the Soviets, because there was also already a movement against the Soviets.
So they wanted everybody in the army.
So I had to go to the army too.
But again, here is Maria Nikitschna.
Another interesting story.
During the time when I was staying with [?
Lonya ?] and [INAUDIBLE], you know, one day Maria Nikitschna comes in and tells me that she was walking on the street and a young woman stopped her, and asked her where she could find to rent a room.
And Maria Nikitschna tells me this was something unusual, you know, and she described to me how she looked, the young woman.
She is blong-- blonde, but I suspect that she's Jewish.
But Maria Nikitschna found her a room-- found her a room and the-- we forgot about that, shall we say.
When we were liberated, we found out this was a Jewish woman.
She was working for the Germans as a nurse, but she was hiding her husband, a doctor, who looked a Jewish.
She was hiding him in that room-- in that room.
So they came to thank Maria Nikitschna, to thank her for that she found the room for her, and et cetera, et cetera.
Then she told-- and this doctor was mobilized by the Soviet right away, but not as a soldier, but as a doctor.
And all the men, you know, had to go there, and he had to say whether they are healthy or not healthy, you know.
So they came to Maria Nikitschna to thank her.
And she said-- told them, I don't want anything from you.
Only thing-- one thing-- I don't want [?
Grisha-- ?] me-- to go to the army, because you could have been killed next day as a soldier, you know.
Then she-- he promised her, when I went to be examined, so he gave me some kind, like six weeks, you know-- like six weeks to get a little healthier, you know.
And then I didn't go to the army, because some of them, you know, I could-- I know two guys who got killed.
So I didn't go to the army.
But now it came out, I thought that I was the only Jew survivor, you know, of the 15000.
And then I found out that there was one, also a Russian woman.
Her name was [?
Sura ?] [?
Tarasova ?], who was rather of loose, shall we say, morals.
But she was hiding eight Jews in her basement.
Her basement.
She was receiving-- she slept with the German officers to get food, but she-- the food was for this Jew-- for the Jewish guys who were there in the basement.
So I found out that I was not the only survivor, and we were there.
And see, they-- they were a little naive.
They went-- she went to the Soviet authorities and expected to get at least some help, you know, because food was scarce [INAUDIBLE].
Then this Soviet secretary there, whatever he was, the-- the mayor [INAUDIBLE], told her, why did you do it?
You didn't have to do it.
So many were killed.
[INAUDIBLE] He was not sympathetic.
But this woman, I called her Saint Magdalen.
I called her Saint Magdalen.
To be a woman like this and do such a thing, you know?
She really slept with the Germans to get food, and the food went down for this-- for the Jewish people.
There was, I think, one woman among them.
Or two women, I don't remember exactly now.
This is one of these rare [INAUDIBLE].
And then from there-- from there, I started to-- to work.
Again, my Russian language, you know, and I started to work.
And then--
Oh, I have statistics, you know.
Statistics [INAUDIBLE].
And what-- I was already free from the-- from the army, you know?
And then when the Russians started moving through-- through-- through the west, you know.
And then we went to-- to Lwow, to Lemberg, you know, Lemberg.
And we arrived there, and then--
I mean together, everybody there again.
I.
I together with the organization already that I worked for, you know.
And then from there, yeah.
Well, there's another interesting thing.
That during-- when I was already liberated, the Polish government-- a new Polish government was created in Moscow.
OK?
A-- a Polish government.
And they were looking for Polish people-- very eminent people, very important people-- to-- to put them into the government.
And in the same city where I was, in Krzemieniec, there was a [?
Rimofski ?].
He was a newspaper man, and they needed him.
So one day, some-- a Polish army was already formed-- formed in Moscow and all over.
And then the two, one Polish general and a woman, the wife of a poet, they came to Krzemieniec to get this guy, [?
Rimofski ?].
This guy, get him.
And I found out about it, and I went and I saw them.
And then I asked for names, you know.
Maybe somebody survived, you know, to get-- and they knew-- and they found one name, a Dr.
Pravin, that was in the army, and I knew him before the war.
And they notified him, and he notified my friend in Moscow that I'm alive, that I survived.
And my friend notified my brother, who was in-- in Asia at that time, in Samarkand, that I was alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This event in events.
So thanks to this--
And then later, I-- when Lodz was liberated, I went to Lodz.
You know?
I went to Lodz and then I started to-- to work there, and I got an apartment.
Oh, by the way, I got an apartment also.
It was not easy to get an apartment.
There are thousands of people standing there before to get an apartment, you see.
And then I met one guy who knew me, knew my brother, and told me that one guy, whose name was [?
Kulesha, ?] is in charge of apartments.
Then I went there, but I couldn't get in.
The poli-- poli-- the policemen outside, they didn't let anybody in unless, you know, he was in the-- in line, you know?
And then I ask him, please, go and tell him.
Just mention my name.
Just mention my name, that I am here outside.
And he did finally.
I convinced him to do it, and that he knew me from before the war, and he gave me a special agent.
He found an apartment for me, so I got an apartment.
I got an apartment, then starts over again a new situation.
I was a free man and-- and-- well, I didn't have anything, clothes to wear.
I didn't have food enough, but already a free man.
And that--
No.
No.
We-- I took it hour by hour, day by day.
And then this guy who was in Moscow, a friend of my brother's and mine, he came later to-- as part of the Polish government.
And he knew about me.
He came, he looked me up.
And then this is old friendship.
The guy is alive and lives in Warsaw now, and his daughter lives here in Cambridge.
His son in law is a-- is for Harvard as a computer man.
And he was, at that time, influential to be sent abroad in diplomatic [INAUDIBLE].
So he-- one day he called me up and asked me whether I would like to go abroad and--
So yes, some of the, yes, as one of them, yes, yes.
And I-- and of course I said yes, and I went first to Turkey.
He sent me to Turkey because they-- they were forming the-- for Turkey.
So I went to Turkey.
And it was-- Turkey was not participating in the war.
So the first impression was on the market, [INAUDIBLE] mountains of oranges, you know?
Food.
Yeah, what an impression, you know?
Because Europe was still hungry.
Still there was not enough food, you know, even after the war.
At first it was very hard.
But Turkey, so I stayed-- we had it very good in a hotel, and I stayed in Turkey.
Went to the capital, Ankara.
We stayed for about two years.
And then I went back to Warsaw.
I was like a-- the [? manager ?] of the office, you know, like a secretary, you know-- secretary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then when I came back to-- to Warsaw, there was a-- a chance to go to-- to-- to the United States.
This was in 1947.
I'm gonna--
Yes, and-- go ahead.
Go ahead.
Yes, I found out that the whole population was killed.
There's only a few somebody in the forest.
There-- there-- Ostrow got forests around there.
They somehow managed.
And one of this-- I met her later in-- in-- in-- in Warsaw and in Lodz.
And she told me the story what happened to her.
She couldn't forgive herself.
She was in a situation in the forest which she was with her husband and her sister.
And there was a situation that she had to choose either-- either the husband or-- or the sister.
And she decided that she notified not her husband to run away, but her sister.
So her sister survived and her husband didn't survive.
And she was very, very, very much di-- disturbed about this-- this decision she made.
But this is the way inhuman situations create.
This-- this whole Holocaust was not something that happened for many thousands of years, never happened anything like that.
So human behavior [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was such that she couldn't believe it, you know?
I can-- I cannot forget the husband, the man, who ran away together with me and left his wife and two children.
I'm sure it did.
Nothing goes over us without leaving an effect.
Even-- even today, this-- this will make an effect on you, and on you, and on you, right?
Nothing-- nothing passes without effect.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You're already-- I'm sure you cannot find a hundred situations that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can-- Well, I can tell you, when-- when I came to-- you know, Warsaw was completely destroyed.
The city was completely destroyed.
Completely destroyed.
So there was a railroad going to Lodz, and I wanted to go to-- to Lodz.
But you couldn't get into-- into the train.
Even on the-- on the roof, was all the people, you couldn't get.
And I was carrying bread, loaves-- two loaves of bread, because they said you couldn't get bread there yet in Lodz.
And I was standing, and I didn't-- I couldn't get to the roof.
I couldn't [INAUDIBLE].
I looked, probably, so terrible, so [LAUGHS] terrible that one came out from the roof down to help me with the bread-- with my sacks [INAUDIBLE].
And I-- and I joined-- there was a big-- a big crowd on the roof.
And then I sat down, and then I hear a remark like this.
Now it is bad.
It is bad.
But at least it is good because there are no Germans and no Jews.
And I had to-- to pretend I didn't hear it.
This was after the war.
[INAUDIBLE] if I had-- I could have-- I don't know if I had said, I am Jewish, he could have thrown me down from the train, you know, at that situation.
So I just didn't say anything.
But the fact that after the war that saw six million people were killed, and a guy like this still has this kind of feeling, you know, this kind of-- of feeling.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had-- I had other situations.
When-- when I was liberated, the first-- the first-- I walked in the Russian, and two Russian colonels, whatever they were, big shots were walking, you know.
And then I-- I walk this way, and they walk, like, the other way.
And one looks at me, and I looked at him.
And one of them was Jewish, you know.
And he said to me that I was the first Jew he met since Stalingrad.
[INAUDIBLE] Stalingrad.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, this was--
But the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there are situations with the-- the Ukrainians.
You see, what they did, their own thing.
They were against Russia, historically.
Historically.
They were occupied for centuries by Russia.
So they expected the Germans to be-- to liberate them, to create a Ukraine.
You know?
Now they have it now, but at that time, they were meeting the-- the Germans with flowers, you know.
But later they convinced themselves that the armies were beating them.
I saw myself one of the German officers beating a Ukrainian policeman.
You know, they-- they-- they-- the Germans had a philosophy.
I was reading there a-- there was a magazine, that was published by Goebbels.
You heard about Goebbels.
He was the minister of propaganda.
And they considered all-- all Slavic nations like slaves for them.
They should work for the Germans, you know.
Oh, this is a-- a German magazine during the occupation.
It was available.
It was available.
It was the Germans.
This was during the occupation.
Nobody.
No-- no normal human being could expect such systematic-- systematic annihilation of-- of-- of six million people.
Somebody could expect there'll be some-- some pogroms.
Some-- some-- you know.
Nobody.
I was always a pessimist.
But I still couldn't expect anything like that, you know?
Nobody could expect anything like that.
But human beings are not human.
What's going on now in-- in Bosnia [INAUDIBLE] and I forget.
Kill each other like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, let's hope that people get a little more humane.
Let's hope.
Thank God, I am glad that I live in a country, that I live-- that I live in America.
Well, I came to this country as a worker for the Polish embassy.
But after three, three and a half years, the situation in Poland became very-- under Stalin, very communistic, very totalitarian, very bad.
And when they told me I had to go back, I-- I decided to stay here.
No.
I never was any political person.
And then I go-- I remember I came to New York because I had somebody who came already after the war.
And I went to-- to-- to a drug store and asked them, said I can type a letter with two fingers.
And I wrote the letter Dean Acheson, who was Secretary of State at that time.

And I wrote a letter, Honor-- Honorable Dean Acheson, Secretary of State, and I told him that I came here as a member of the Polish embassy, that now I am supposed to go back, but I asked for political asylum.
And I added, I am not nor have I ever been a member of the Communist Party.
So I became-- I became American citizen by the Act-- a special Act of the Congress.
Yeah.
Because I asked for political asylum it took time, but one day, I knew when I was in Washington, I learned English much better, and I-- I got friends, you know.
One of them was working as a secretary for a Senator Saltonstall from Massachusetts-- oh, from Massachusetts.
And he called me up and said, Gregory, you're-- you're authorized-- you're becoming citizen by the Congress, because he saw the papers, you know?
And then, after a time, they called me and I-- I got my citizenship.
And here I am.
